story:
I swung by West 4th Street late, after filing the Farrell column, just to see my old friend Pipes. We grew up downtown, where the old streets tangle and the cobblestones peek through the blacktop, and even in 1962—with Corvettes on the highways and the Cold War in the headlines—the Village stayed a mix of art, protest, and a little mob at the corners. Pipes—Angelo to me—had started as a bouncer, short and thick as a fire plug, the kind of strong that didn’t need to show off. He kept order, got himself ten percent of the club, and sang when he felt like it. That’s how he got the nickname.

He was in his tux under the red awning trimmed with a whisper of tinsel. “Just a little,” he said, proud and shy at the same time. We traded the usual wisecracks, and he asked if I had anyone for the season. I didn’t. He did, though; he just didn’t want to say it. Verna was back—tall, silver, her voice warm as cream—and when they sang together, he played piano soft and let her have the lead. You didn’t need to be a mind reader. I could hear how he looked at her.

The club was one of those carved-up townhouses—tables wherever they’d fit, French doors on an overflow no one used. A waiter whispered that the local sergeant was getting loud. Pipes raised a hand and went over calm. The cop stood up unsteady, talked tough, then swung. Pipes was faster. One clean shot, the sergeant into the glass, splinters and shards everywhere. Pipes’ guys hauled the cop out. The crew swept up. Nobody got hurt worse than pride, and Pipes rubbed his knuckles like he’d just fixed a hinge. “He’ll sober up and want back in,” he said. “I’ll let him.” Keeping the block safe mattered, and he did it without showing off.

We were back to Verna. “You’re in love,” I told him. He didn’t say yes, not at first. Then he did. We stepped outside, and there she was under the awning, silver gown catching the soft light, no car in sight. Vito the valet hovered, useless.

“Tell her,” I said.

“She’s a head taller,” he said. “What would she want with a little guy like me?”

Verna turned, watching us. I kept quiet. Pipes went down to her. “What are you waiting for?”

“I don’t have a ride,” she said.

“You always have a ride.”

“Not tonight.” She dipped at the knees so that silver brushed the sidewalk, just enough to meet him a little closer. “I was hoping you would. I need help putting the star on the tree.”

Pipes’ smile took his whole face. “Yeah, Verna. Vito, the car.” He offered an elbow; she slipped an arm around his shoulder and gave him a quick, soft hug. They slid into his new Thunderbird and waved off into a city that, for a night, had no trouble at all. That was their Christmas present.

In January they sang together like they’d always meant to. In February she moved into his tidy top-floor place over the club. The few street trees put out tiny green buds, and their love lit the neighborhood warmer than any neon. Then one warm night a jealous man stepped from the shadows under the red awning and shot them both. Quick, senseless.

I miss my friend. Verna was the bright star in his sky, and for a little while they lit West 4th Street with a true, sweet glow. Not long, no. But more than many ever find in little old New York.
